name: Michoacán Mexico Avocado Belt

staple_foods.name: avocado oil (Hass, highland Michoacán)

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 65.8-73.4% of total fatty acids [Hass avocado oil, Michoacán highland 1700m — Flores et al. 2019 Food Chem 290:90; highest oleic at highland temperatures]
primary_metabolites.linoleic_acid: 10.5-14.2% of total fatty acids [Michoacán Hass avocado oil — Flores et al. 2019]
primary_metabolites.palmitic_acid: 11.8-14.5% of total fatty acids [Michoacán avocado oil — Flores et al. 2019]
primary_metabolites.palmitoleic_acid: 3.5-6.0% of total fatty acids [avocado oil — notable palmitoleic content; Flores et al. 2019]

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 180-320 mg GAE/100g [Michoacán avocado oil — Flores et al. 2019; higher in highland-grown vs coastal avocado]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 90-160 mg/kg [Michoacán Hass avocado oil — Flores et al. 2019; beta- and gamma-tocopherol significant]
key_flavor_bioactives.hydroxytyrosol: absent — avocado oil lacks olive-specific phenolics

staple_foods.notes: Michoacán Highland Hass avocado oil — the highest-altitude and highest-oleic anchor of the avocado oil natural experiment. Michoacán State produces ~60% of global avocado supply. The highland elevation (~1700m) and cooler growing temperature (~18.5°C) are associated with significantly elevated oleic acid proportion (65-73%) compared to coastal/lower-altitude production. Flores et al. (2019) demonstrate this altitude-ripening-temperature gradient in Hass oleic acid. Natural experiment pair with RSU-44 (California coast, ~150m, lower altitude/warmer). The palmitoleic acid content (~3.5-6%) is notable and unique among plant oils.
data_sources: Flores et al. (2019) — Effect of altitude on avocado oil fatty acid composition, Food Chem 290:90

staple_foods.name: nixtamalized corn (maize tortilla)

primary_metabolites.amino_acid_content: niacin bioavailability dramatically increased by nixtamalization alkaline treatment

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 200-500 mg GAE/100g [nixtamalized blue/red corn — hydroxycinnamic acids dominant; Adom & Liu 2002]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: trace [masa — minimal LAB activity in nixtamal process]

staple_foods.notes: Nixtamalized corn tortilla — the foundational carbohydrate of Mesoamerican food culture. The nixtamalization process (alkaline lime-water soaking) is one of the most important food processing innovations in human history: it releases niacin, increases calcium, and transforms corn flavor. Michoacán highland criollo landrace varieties (Olotillo, Olotón, Tabloncillo) are distinct from lowland varieties.
data_sources: Adom & Liu (2002) — Antioxidant activity of grains, J Agric Food Chem 50:6182

staple_foods.name: black beans (frijoles negros)

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 1500-3500 mg GAE/100g [black bean seed coat anthocyanins + tannins — Tsuda et al. 1994 Biosci Biotechnol Biochem 58:1780]
key_flavor_bioactives.tannin_content: 3-8 mg CE/g [black bean condensed tannins — Tsuda et al. 1994]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 2.0-2.8 g/100g free glutamate [cooked black beans — glutamic acid dominant legume amino acid; USDA FDC 173734]

staple_foods.notes: Black beans — the protein staple of the Michoacán milpa system (corn-beans-squash polyculture). The black seed coat anthocyanins give exceptionally high polyphenol content. Glutamic acid is the dominant amino acid, contributing to the earthy-savory flavor of bean broth (caldo de frijoles).
data_sources: Tsuda et al. (1994) — Antioxidative pigments isolated from black bean seed coat, Biosci Biotechnol Biochem 58:1780

staple_foods.name: pulque (fermented agave sap)

primary_metabolites.glucose_concentration: 1-3 g/100g [pulque — residual sugars after fermentation; Escalante et al. 2016 Sci Rep 6:29068]
primary_metabolites.fructose_concentration: 0.5-2.0 g/100g [pulque — fructans partially hydrolyzed; Escalante et al. 2016]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.5-1.5 g/100g [pulque — LAB produce lactic acid alongside yeast; Escalante et al. 2016]
organic_acids.acetic_acid: 0.2-0.6 g/100g [pulque — heterofermentative bacteria; Escalante et al. 2016]

staple_foods.notes: Pulque — the traditional fermented beverage of highland central Mexico, produced from the aquamiel (honey water) of the maguey agave. One of the oldest fermented beverages in the Americas — complex microbiome (LAB, Zymomonas, yeasts) produces lactic acid, ethanol, and a viscous, slightly sour, yeasty-funky profile. At low alcohol (~4-6% ABV), pulque retains the prebiotic fructan content of agave alongside organic acids. Used as daily staple drink in Michoacán/Hidalgo highlands for millennia.
data_sources: Escalante et al. (2016) — Pulque microbial diversity, Sci Rep 6:29068

staple_foods.name: chile ancho / mulato (dried chile)

primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: 10-20 mg/100g [dried chile — significant loss during drying from fresh levels]

key_flavor_bioactives.capsaicinoids: 0.5-2.5 mg/g [ancho chile dried — moderate heat, 1000-2000 Scoville; Ornelas-Paz et al. 2010 J Agric Food Chem 58:3234]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 3000-6000 mg GAE/100g [dried ancho chile — carotenoids + phenolics; Ornelas-Paz et al. 2010]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 20-60 mg/100g [dried chile peppers — alpha-tocopherol; Ornelas-Paz et al. 2010]

staple_foods.notes: Ancho/Mulato dried chile — the aromatic foundation of Michoacán mole and chile sauce tradition. Drying concentrates capsaicinoids and carotenoids dramatically. The multi-layer flavor of dried ancho (fruity, earthy, mild-moderate heat) vs fresh chile is one of the most transformed flavor profiles in Mexican cuisine. Michoacán-grown highland chiles develop at elevated altitude, potentially with elevated UV-driven polyphenol accumulation.
data_sources: Ornelas-Paz et al. (2010) — Composition of capsaicinoids and antioxidants in Mexican chiles, J Agric Food Chem 58:3234

notes: Michoacán State, Mexico (~1700m), volcanic Trans-Mexican Volcanic Belt. Acidic volcanic loam soils, highland subtropical climate with strong seasonal rain. The defining fat terroir signal is the altitude-elevated oleic acid in Hass avocado oil — the best-documented altitude effect on fruit fat composition in the dataset after olive oil. Natural experiment pair with RSU-44 (California coast, ~150m). The milpa system (corn-beans-chile polyculture) plus pulque fermentation complete a culturally coherent and metabolically rich food system. Nixtamalization is one of the most nutritionally transformative traditional processing methods in the global food dataset.
